text: The thoracoscopic esophagectomy is performed with the patient in the prone position using a three-port technique. The opti-view entry is established, and the intra-thoracic pressure is set to eight to ten millimeters of mercury.

The surgeon opens the inferior pulmonary ligament, followed by the mediastinal pleura, revealing the inferior pulmonary vein. The right vagus nerve is visible near the vein, indicating the location for further dissection.

The surgeon opens the mediastinal pleura above the level of the right vagus nerve, then proceeds to open the posterior mediastinal pleura, taking care to avoid injury to the azygos vein and thoracic duct. The right bronchus is visible and safeguarded during the procedure.
The video shows the tumor located on the left side of the esophagus, with the esophagus partially obscured by the prominent vertebra.
The surgeon ties around the esophagus and uses an additional port to apply traction, rotating the esophagus by 90 degrees to bring the tumor into view.
The surgeon opens the longitudinal fibers of the esophagus to access the tumor plane, revealing the hard tumor within the esophageal tissue.
The surgeon places a suture on the upper part of the tumor and applies traction, facilitating the exposure of the tumor and the surrounding plane for further dissection.

The surgeon continues the resection using a bipolar artery device, ensuring there is no active bleeding. Traction is applied to the idiomyoma to maintain the correct surgical plane. The surgeon carefully bipolarizes small peaks while avoiding the use of a monopolar artery and ensuring the mucosa remains uninjured.

The surgeon approximates the separated longitudinal fibers of the esophagus using interrupted sutures with 2-0 Vicryl, ensuring the sutures are not too tight to avoid mucosal injury or narrowing of the esophagus.

The surgeon performs an apogee endoscopy to verify the integrity of the esophageal mucosa, ensuring the endoscope passes smoothly without obstruction. An air leak test is conducted to confirm the absence of air leaks, marking the completion of the surgery.
